title: Nausea and transaminases in the thousands in a patient on multiple hepatotoxins

chart.oneLiner: 34F with depression, chronic low back pain, and treated latent TB presents with 3 days of nausea, vomiting, malaise, and RUQ pain; she takes several over-the-counter and prescription products for pain and recently had a stretch of poor oral intake. She looks pale and slightly tachycardic, and her transaminases come back in the thousands.

opening: I just can't stop feeling sick to my stomach, and my upper belly is sore.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old woman, nauseated, pale, tired, somewhat guarded about how much medication she has been taking and about her mood. Becomes more forthcoming when asked directly and nonjudgmentally.

HPI: About 3 days of nausea, frequent vomiting, malaise, decreased appetite, and a dull ache under the right ribs. She has had a flare of low back pain and headaches for over a week and has been 'taking whatever helps' — including extra-strength acetaminophen tablets AND a prescription acetaminophen/oxycodone (Percocet) combination, often together, not realizing both contain the same pain reliever (a staggered, supratherapeutic, therapeutic-misadventure pattern over several days rather than one big overdose). Because she felt so sick she barely ate for two days (poor intake / relative fasting). On sensitive, specific questioning she also discloses low mood and that 3 days ago, during a bad evening, she did take a larger handful of the tablets 'to make the pain and everything stop' — she is vague about exact count and ambivalent about intent. She drinks alcohol most evenings (about 3-4 drinks/night) but did NOT have a discrete binge. NO black/bloody stools, NO recent hypotensive event or fainting that she recalls (relevant to shock liver), NO known sick contacts, NO IV drug use, NO new tattoos, NO mushroom foraging, NO herbal weight-loss teas — but she does take St. John's Wort she bought for her mood.
PMH (reveal when asked): major depression and anxiety; chronic low back pain; latent TB diagnosed on pre-employment screening, currently on isoniazid. NO known prior liver disease, NO hepatitis. MEDS (reveal when asked, and EMPHASIZE if asked to list everything she takes for pain or 'anything over the counter or herbal'): extra-strength acetaminophen (multiple tablets/day for days), acetaminophen/oxycodone (Percocet) for back pain, ibuprofen, citalopram for depression, isoniazid for latent TB, and over-the-counter St. John's Wort. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: lives alone; drinks alcohol most nights (3-4 drinks); smokes occasionally; no recreational injection drugs. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: nausea, vomiting, anorexia, malaise, RUQ discomfort; no fever/chills, no jaundice noticed by her, no confusion noted by her (but assess for it).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, nauseated, mildly diaphoretic, alert and oriented.
- Vitals: BP 104/64, HR 112, RR 20, 37.4 C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Mucous membranes/skin: dry mucous membranes, mild scleral icterus if specifically inspected; skin slightly cool.
- Abdomen: tender RIGHT upper quadrant over the liver edge, which is mildly enlarged and tender; no rebound, no rigidity.
- Neuro: alert; subtle/equivocal asterixis if specifically examined (early, ominous — flag it); otherwise no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the total amount of acetaminophen, that two different products both contain acetaminophen, the timing, the intent, the alcohol amount, the isoniazid, or the St. John's Wort unless the student asks specifically (how much, when, why, what else including prescription/over-the-counter/herbal, alcohol).
- Approach mood and intent nonjudgmentally; do not provide details about methods of self-harm.
- Reveal exam findings (icterus, hepatomegaly/tenderness, asterixis) only when that exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer. Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.Acetaminophen: Serum acetaminophen level 142 mcg/mL. Because this is a STAGGERED ingestion over several days with an uncertain last-dose time, the Rumack-Matthew nomogram does NOT apply — treat based on risk (detectable level + transaminitis), do not be falsely reassured by a single value.
labs.LFTs: AST 6,820 U/L, ALT 5,540 U/L (AST > ALT, both in the thousands — massive centrilobular hepatocellular injury). Total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase mildly elevated. Pattern is hepatocellular, not cholestatic.
labs.INR: INR 2.6 and rising on repeat (baseline normal) — synthetic dysfunction / coagulopathy, a King's College prognostic marker.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 100, HCO3 16 (anion gap = 138 - (100 + 16) = 22, elevated). Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline ~0.8 — acute kidney injury). Glucose 58 mg/dL (hypoglycemia from failing hepatic gluconeogenesis). BUN 24.
labs.ABG_Lactate: pH 7.28, pCO2 30, HCO3 15, base deficit present; lactate 4.5 mmol/L — high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with hyperlactatemia (severity/prognostic marker; arterial pH < 7.30 is a King's College criterion).
labs.Coingestants: Salicylate level negative; ethanol detectable but low; urine tox otherwise unremarkable. Viral hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg/anti-HBc IgM, HCV Ab) and acetaminophen-protein adduct sent — viral serologies negative, supporting acetaminophen over acute viral hepatitis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~112 bpm with a prolonged QT interval (QTc ~490 ms) and no acute ST-elevation; the prolonged QT is driven by her metabolic derangement (acidosis, AKI) plus QT-prolonging citalopram.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with drug/metabolic QT prolongation, no acute ST elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval
ekg.clinicalVignette: A patient with metabolic acidosis, acute kidney injury, and a QT-prolonging antidepressant has sinus tachycardia and a long QT on the tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Small dense calcified granuloma with possible calcified hilar node (old healed granulomatous disease) — an incidental, benign finding that fits her history of latent TB.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Small dense calcified granuloma with possible calcified hilar node (old healed granulomatous disease). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.clinicalVignette: A patient with a history of latent TB has a routine film showing an incidental dense calcified granuloma with a calcified hilar node.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis). Explicitly name THREE causes of AST/ALT in the thousands that you are weighing here.
steps.rubric: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) hepatotoxicity — leading & CANT-MISS, given the staggered supratherapeutic ingestion across two acetaminophen-containing products plus risk modifiers (CANT-MISS,8). Ischemic hepatitis / 'shock liver' — genuine competitor given tachycardia, low-normal BP, vomiting, and poor intake; AST/ALT also in the thousands (CANT-MISS,4). Acute viral hepatitis — must exclude with serologies (core,3). Other drug/toxin hepatotoxicity (isoniazid, herbal St. John's Wort) as a contributor/confounder (core,2). Alcoholic hepatitis — typically AST:ALT 2:1 but transaminases rarely in the thousands, so less likely (core,2). Names THREE causes of transaminases in the thousands (acetaminophen/toxin, ischemic hepatitis, acute viral hepatitis) (core,3). Connects ingestion history + transaminases in the thousands + rising INR to acetaminophen toxicity and the need for a level regardless of the nomogram (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on acetaminophen without considering ischemic hepatitis or viral hepatitis as competitors (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) hepatotoxicity — a staggered, supratherapeutic ingestion across TWO acetaminophen-containing products (extra-strength acetaminophen + acetaminophen/oxycodone), amplified by risk modifiers (chronic alcohol use, relative fasting/poor intake, isoniazid, St. John's Wort). The three causes of AST/ALT in the thousands I am actively weighing: (1) acetaminophen/toxin-induced hepatotoxicity, (2) ischemic hepatitis ('shock liver') — she is tachycardic, low-normal BP, vomiting and poorly perfused, and (3) acute viral hepatitis. Also reasonable/contributory: other drug/herbal hepatotoxins (isoniazid, St. John's Wort); alcoholic hepatitis is less likely because it rarely drives transaminases into the thousands. A serum acetaminophen level is mandatory even though a single-timepoint nomogram cannot be applied to a staggered ingestion.

coreActions.item: Other drug/toxin hepatotoxicity (isoniazid, St. John's Wort)
Names three causes of transaminases in the thousands
Connects ingestion history + transaminases/INR to acetaminophen toxicity and the need for a level

penalties.item: Anchors on acetaminophen without considering ischemic hepatitis or viral hepatitis

acceptableAlternatives: Other drug/toxin hepatotoxicity (isoniazid, St. John's Wort)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What is the single most important level to check, and what else do you order to sort out this multi-system picture?
steps.rubric: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion (CANT-MISS,4). LFTs with bilirubin (2). INR/coags (2). BMP — renal function, glucose, anion gap (2). ABG/lactate for severity (1). Salicylate level + co-ingestant screen + viral hepatitis serologies (2). Recognizes a single level cannot be plotted on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram for a staggered/unknown-time ingestion (1). Checks EKG given QT-prolonging citalopram + metabolic derangement; CXR (1). PENALTY: not checking an acetaminophen level (-3); relying on the nomogram to clear a staggered ingestion (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Single most important: a serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with the timing/pattern of ingestion (it confirms exposure even though a staggered ingestion cannot be plotted on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram). Also order: LFTs with bilirubin; INR/coags; BMP (renal function, glucose, anion gap); ABG with lactate; salicylate level and co-ingestant screen; viral hepatitis serologies (HAV IgM, HBsAg/anti-HBc IgM, HCV Ab) to address the viral competitor; EKG (she is on QT-prolonging citalopram and is acidotic/AKI) and CXR.

criticalActions.item: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion

penalties.item: relying on the nomogram to clear a staggered ingestion

requiredTests: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion
contraindicatedTests: relying on the nomogram to clear a staggered ingestion
orderSensitiveActions: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and their relevance to her presentation.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia (~112 bpm) with a PROLONGED QT interval (QTc ~490 ms) and no acute ST elevation. CREDIT: systematic rate & rhythm read identifying sinus tachycardia (5); identifies the prolonged QT (5); attributes it to the metabolic derangement (acidosis/AKI) plus QT-prolonging citalopram and flags torsades risk (3). PENALTY: missing the long QT, or over-reading the tracing as a primary acute coronary event (-3).
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval; no acute ST elevation.
commonMistakes: over-reading sinus tachycardia as a primary acute coronary event
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~112 bpm with a prolonged QT interval (QTc ~490 ms) and no acute ST elevation. The long QT is multifactorial here — metabolic acidosis and acute kidney injury plus the QT-prolonging antidepressant citalopram — and it raises the risk of torsades, so she needs telemetry, electrolyte repletion (K/Mg), and avoidance of further QT-prolonging drugs.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval; no acute ST elevation.
bonusActions: attributes the long QT to acidosis/AKI plus citalopram and flags torsades risk

penalties.label: misses the prolonged QT or over-reads as a primary acute coronary event

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = small dense calcified granuloma (benign, old healed granulomatous disease), fitting her latent-TB history. CREDIT: identifies the calcified granuloma (6); recognizes it as a benign healed finding, not malignancy (4). PENALTY: working up a clearly calcified benign nodule as cancer, or calling the study normal / missing the finding (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes it as a benign healed finding
commonMistakes: working up a clearly calcified benign nodule as malignancy
steps.idealAnswer: Calcified granuloma — a benign sequela of old granulomatous infection (consistent with her latent-TB history); dense uniform calcification, no aggressive features and no need for malignancy workup.

coreActions: recognizes it as a benign healed finding

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The acetaminophen level is detectable with AST/ALT in the thousands, INR 2.6 and rising, anion-gap acidosis, AKI, and hypoglycemia. Commit to a diagnosis, name the discriminating data that resolve it over the competitors, and list THREE factors that potentiated her acetaminophen toxicity. What predicts a poor outcome?
steps.rubric: Acetaminophen-induced acute liver injury / early acute liver failure (6). Discriminators: detectable acetaminophen level with hepatocellular AST>ALT pattern and negative viral serologies (favors acetaminophen over viral hepatitis); no documented hypotensive/shock event and the dominant exposure history (favors acetaminophen over ischemic hepatitis) (2). Names THREE potentiating factors (chronic alcohol use, fasting/poor intake, isoniazid, St. John's Wort — all deplete glutathione or induce CYP2E1 → more NAPQI) (1). Names poor-prognosis markers — rising INR, arterial pH < 7.30 / high lactate, AKI/creatinine, encephalopathy (King's College criteria) (1).
steps.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: Acetaminophen-induced acute liver injury progressing toward acute liver failure. Discriminating data: a detectable acetaminophen level with a hepatocellular pattern (AST 6,820 > ALT 5,540, both in the thousands) and NEGATIVE viral hepatitis serologies argue against acute viral hepatitis; the absence of a documented hypotensive/shock event together with the clear staggered overdose history argues against ischemic hepatitis (though both remain on the differential until viral serologies and the clinical course confirm). Three factors that potentiated her toxicity: (1) chronic alcohol use (induces CYP2E1 and depletes glutathione), (2) fasting/poor oral intake for days (glutathione depletion), and (3) concurrent CYP-inducing/hepatotoxic drugs — isoniazid and St. John's Wort — all of which increase toxic NAPQI. Poor-prognosis markers (King's College criteria for acetaminophen): arterial pH < 7.30 (hers is 7.28) OR the triad of INR > 6.5, creatinine > 3.4, and grade III/IV encephalopathy; her rising INR (2.6), high lactate (4.5), AKI, and any encephalopathy/asterixis are ominous.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including monitoring and the correct disposition? Is this patient safe to discharge?
steps.rubric: Per MGH acute liver failure (APAP): N-acetylcysteine, give empirically and WITHOUT delay for a toxic ingestion / transaminases in the thousands / rising level — do not wait for or rely on the nomogram in a staggered ingestion (CANT-MISS,8); IV dextrose for hypoglycemia + IV fluids for AKI/perfusion (3); telemetry with electrolyte (K/Mg) repletion and avoid further QT-prolonging drugs given the long QT (3); serial monitoring of INR/pH/Cr/lactate/glucose/mental status — King's College criteria (3); early hepatology/transplant-center referral and Poison Control/toxicology (3); correct disposition — ADMIT (ICU if ALF), do NOT discharge; medical psychiatry/risk assessment and one-to-one observation before any future discharge given the intentional component (3); medication reconciliation and counseling to stop all acetaminophen-containing products, isoniazid, and St. John's Wort, with safe-discharge planning only once medically and psychiatrically cleared (2). PENALTY: delaying NAC awaiting a nomogram/level in a late or staggered ingestion (-4); discharging the patient or missing the suicide-risk/psychiatric disposition (-4).
steps.idealAnswer: Give N-ACETYLCYSTEINE empirically and immediately — a detectable level with transaminases in the thousands and rising INR mandates NAC now; do NOT wait on or rely on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, which cannot be applied to a staggered ingestion. Treat the multi-system derangement: IV dextrose for hypoglycemia (glucose 58) and IV fluids for the AKI and perfusion; place on telemetry, replete potassium and magnesium, and avoid additional QT-prolonging agents because of the prolonged QT (hold citalopram). Serially monitor INR, arterial pH, creatinine, lactate, glucose, and mental status and apply the King's College criteria; involve Poison Control/toxicology early and refer to hepatology / a transplant center for acute liver failure. DISPOSITION: she is NOT safe for discharge — admit (ICU if acute liver failure). Because the ingestion had an intentional/self-harm component, obtain an urgent psychiatric/risk assessment with one-to-one observation and a safe environment. Reconcile her medications and counsel her to stop all acetaminophen-containing products (she was double-dosing across two products), and discuss isoniazid and St. John's Wort with her physicians. Safe discharge is planned only once she is medically AND psychiatrically cleared, with close follow-up.

coreActions.item: IV dextrose for hypoglycemia and IV fluids for AKI/perfusion
Telemetry, replete K/Mg, avoid further QT-prolonging drugs (hold citalopram)
Monitor INR/pH/Cr/lactate/glucose and mental status (King's College)
Correct disposition: admit (ICU if ALF), do not discharge; urgent psychiatric/risk assessment with one-to-one observation
Medication reconciliation and counseling (stop acetaminophen products; safe-discharge planning only when medically and psychiatrically cleared)

penalties.item: Delaying NAC awaiting a nomogram/level in a late or staggered ingestion
Discharging the patient or missing the suicide-risk/psychiatric disposition

optionalActions: IV dextrose for hypoglycemia and IV fluids for AKI/perfusion
Telemetry, replete K/Mg, avoid further QT-prolonging drugs (hold citalopram)
Monitor INR/pH/Cr/lactate/glucose and mental status (King's College)
Correct disposition: admit (ICU if ALF), do not discharge; urgent psychiatric/risk assessment with one-to-one observation
Medication reconciliation and counseling (stop acetaminophen products; safe-discharge planning only when medically and psychiatrically cleared)
unsafeActions: Delaying NAC awaiting a nomogram/level in a late or staggered ingestion
Discharging the patient or missing the suicide-risk/psychiatric disposition
steps.disposition: Admit; ICU if ALF; one-to-one observation; do not discharge until medically and psychiatrically cleared

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: RIGHT upper quadrant tenderness over a mildly enlarged, tender liver edge; no rebound, no rigidity.
Abdomen: RIGHT upper quadrant tenderness over a mildly enlarged, tender liver edge; no rebound, no rigidity.
Mild scleral icterus on close inspection.
Dry mucous membranes; skin slightly cool (volume depletion / hypoperfusion).
Neuro: subtle/equivocal asterixis on examination (early encephalopathy, an ominous sign).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34F with depression, chronic low back pain, treated latent TB, and chronic alcohol use presents with 3 days of nausea, vomiting, malaise, and RUQ pain after a staggered, supratherapeutic acetaminophen ingestion across TWO acetaminophen-containing products, with a partly intentional component. The diagnosis is Acetaminophen-induced liver injury / early acute liver failure, reached by eliciting the sensitive medication and mood history, recognizing transaminases in the thousands with rising INR, anion-gap acidosis, AKI, and hypoglycemia as a multi-system toxic picture, and resolving it over the competing causes of transaminases in the thousands (ischemic hepatitis, acute viral hepatitis).
keyHistoryClues: anything else you take including over the counter or herbal
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: RIGHT upper quadrant tenderness over a mildly enlarged, tender liver edge; no rebound, no rigidity.
Mild scleral icterus on close inspection.
Neuro: subtle/equivocal asterixis on examination (early encephalopathy, an ominous sign).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (tachycardia, low-normal BP, pallor).
Elicit the focused history, including the sensitive medication-overuse, alcohol, and mood/intent items only on direct, nonjudgmental questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam (RUQ/hepatomegaly, scleral icterus, asterixis) to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by acetaminophen hepatotoxicity while naming the competing causes of transaminases in the thousands (ischemic hepatitis, acute viral hepatitis).
Order an acetaminophen level (recognizing the nomogram cannot clear a staggered ingestion) plus a multi-system workup, and integrate the EKG (sinus tachycardia with long QT) and CXR.
Commit to acetaminophen-induced acute liver injury/failure, start NAC empirically, manage the multi-system derangement, and arrange the correct admit/psychiatric disposition rather than discharge.
commonPitfalls: Delaying NAC while waiting on the nomogram/level in a staggered ingestion
Anchoring on acetaminophen without weighing ischemic or viral hepatitis
Discharging the patient or missing the suicide-risk/psychiatric disposition
managementPearls: Correct disposition: admit (ICU if ALF), one-to-one observation, do not discharge
criticalWorkupItems: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval; no acute ST elevation.
criticalManagementItems: Correct disposition: admit (ICU if ALF), do not discharge; urgent psychiatric/risk assessment with one-to-one observation
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval; no acute ST elevation.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval; no acute ST elevation.
imageInterpretationPearls: A prolonged QT in an acidotic, AKI patient on citalopram is the patient's own study — name it, attribute it, and act on torsades risk.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions about mood and intent
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language

libraryUpgrade.imagePolicy: integrated patient-specific EKG read; standalone CXR interpretation
